common_name: Forget Me Anthurium (Forgetii, Silver Vein Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or greenhouse specimen in bright indirect light with high humidity (60–80%); Anthurium forgetii is a compact collector's anthurium that performs well as a tabletop specimen. Use a chunky, well-aerated epiphytic mix of orchid bark, perlite, and potting soil. Water thoroughly when the top layer dries. Propagate from stem cuttings with an aerial root or from seed. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Anthurium forgetii produces its best foliage with consistent bright indirect light and will slow considerably in dim conditions. Anthurium forgetii is a medium-sized velvet anthurium native to the department of Chocó in Colombia, closely related to Anthurium crystallinum and Anthurium magnificum but immediately distinguished by one uniquely diagnostic feature: unlike virtually every other commonly grown anthurium, the leaves of A. forgetii have no sinus — the two basal lobes of the otherwise heart-shaped leaf are pressed together or slightly overlapping, creating a completely closed, teardrop-shaped leaf base with no open notch. In all other respects, the leaves are classically beautiful: velvety, dark emerald-green, with bold, bright silver-white primary veins. The closed leaf base and generally more compact growth habit (compared to the giant velvet anthuriums like A. veitchii and A. warocqueanum) make A. forgetii a more practical choice for collectors with limited space. It produces the characteristic Anthurium flowers (green-white spathe and spadix) that are ornamentally inconsequential. Anthurium forgetii has become an important parent in hybrid anthurium breeding, contributing the closed-sinus trait to offspring. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium forgetii species notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Anthurium collector species cultivation
American Horticulture Society — Velvet anthuriums: collector species guide
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to pets)

common_name: Dragon Tail Plant (Centipede Tongavine, Cebu Blue Pothos, Tibatib)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant, hanging basket, or shaded climbing vine in zone 9; Epipremnum pinnatum cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. Provide a moss pole or climbing surface to achieve the spectacular adult leaf form — trailing specimens maintain the smaller, silvery-blue juvenile 'Cebu Blue' leaves. In zone 10-11, it grows vigorously outdoors in deep shade, climbing tree trunks to produce enormous adult leaves. Propagate readily from single-node stem cuttings. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Dragon Tail Plant adapts to lower light in its juvenile form but requires adequate light and a vertical climbing support to progress to its spectacular adult leaf form. Epipremnum pinnatum is a large, climbing aroid vine native to Southeast Asia, the Pacific Islands, and northern Australia, distributed across an enormous range from India to Polynesia in the Araceae family. The plant exhibits one of the most dramatic cases of leaf dimorphism in common cultivation: in the juvenile trailing or hanging form, it produces narrow, elongated, slightly iridescent, silvery-blue-green leaves 4–8 inches long with no lobing — the form sold commercially as 'Cebu Blue Pothos' (though not a true Pothos). When the same plant begins climbing a rough surface and receives adequate light, it transitions progressively to the adult form: leaves become dramatically larger (up to 24–36 inches), dark green, and deeply pinnately lobed along the midrib in a pattern resembling a dragon's tail or centipede — giving rise to the common name 'Dragon Tail Plant.' The adult leaves develop fenestrations along the midrib similar to but distinct from Monstera. The transformation from slender blue juvenile to enormous pinnate adult is dramatic and rewarding for collectors who provide climbing support. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Epipremnum pinnatum species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Epipremnum species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Aroid leaf dimorphism: juvenile vs. adult forms
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Epipremnum (toxic to pets)

common_name: Dragon Tail Rhaphidophora (Creeping Philodendron, Monster Dragon Tail)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or climbing container vine in zone 9; Rhaphidophora decursiva cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. A moss pole or coco coir totem is strongly recommended — the plant climbs rapidly and produces successively larger, more lobed leaves as it ascends. In zone 10-11 outdoors, it can climb large trees in shaded positions. Propagate from stem cuttings with at least one node and aerial root. Use a well-aerated aroid potting mix. All parts toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Rhaphidophora decursiva transitions from attractive juvenile form to spectacular adult form most quickly in bright indirect light with a climbing support, remaining in the juvenile stage indefinitely if conditions prevent climbing. Rhaphidophora decursiva is a vigorous climbing aroid from South and Southeast Asia (India, Sri Lanka, southern China, and across to the Philippines and Indonesia) in the family Araceae — not a Philodendron or Monstera despite being commonly mislabeled as both in commercial plant sales. The juvenile form produces simple, elliptical, glossy, dark-green leaves that are attractive but unremarkable. As the plant matures and climbs upward, the leaves transform progressively and dramatically: they enlarge significantly and develop deep pinnate lobing — the leaf divides into a row of distinct, finger-like segments radiating from the central midrib — eventually producing 24-inch leaves deeply cut almost to the midrib in a pattern resembling an enormous feather or a prehistoric wing, from which the common name 'Dragon Tail' derives. This adult form is often confused with Epipremnum pinnatum in its adult form (also called 'Dragon Tail') and with large pinnate Monstera species — all three can produce superficially similar adult leaves. Rhaphidophora decursiva is a fast grower that rewards patience with an extraordinary final form. All parts are toxic due to calcium oxalate.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Rhaphidophora decursiva species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rhaphidophora species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Collector aroids: Rhaphidophora identification
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Rhaphidophora (toxic to pets)

common_name: Dwarf Jamaican Heliconia (Firebird Heliconia, Stricta Heliconia)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring when soil temperature reaches 65°F+ in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Heliconia stricta grows best in the dappled light of tropical-style beds and shaded ornamental borders. In zone 9, the rhizomes survive mild winters underground — mulch heavily in zone 9a to protect roots from hard frost. The 'Dwarf Jamaican' and 'Firebird' cultivars are among the most compact and zone-9-tolerant selections. Divide clumps every 3–4 years. The upright, scarlet to orange-red bracts are excellent for tropical cut flower arrangements.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Heliconia stricta grows and blooms best with morning sun and afternoon shade in zone 9's intense climate, where full-day sun combined with heat and dryness can stress the large paddle-shaped leaves. Heliconia stricta is one of the most widely grown and zone-adaptable of the heliconias, a species from the tropical forests of Central and South America and the Caribbean (Trinidad, Venezuela, Colombia, Brazil) that has given rise to numerous popular garden cultivars. The plant produces the characteristic banana-like paddle foliage on tall upright stems, topped by the distinctive erect, boat-shaped bracts: in the species and most cultivars, the bracts are waxy, bright scarlet to orange-red, arranged in an upright raceme and persist for weeks as cut flowers or on the plant. Popular named cultivars include 'Dwarf Jamaican' (compact form with red-orange bracts), 'Firebird' (vivid orange-red bracts), and 'Sharonii' (scarlet bracts with green tips). Heliconia stricta is significantly more compact than the giant species (H. bihai, H. caribaea) that can exceed 15 feet, making it practical for residential zone 9 tropical garden beds. The flowers are excellent hummingbird attractants. In zone 9a, rhizomes usually survive mild winters with heavy mulch protection.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heliconia stricta for Florida tropical gardens
LSU AgCenter — Heliconia species for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Heliconia stricta cultivars
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical ornamentals for Gulf Coast zone 9

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in partial shade to full sun with well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Mexican Honeysuckle grows rapidly in zone 9 and begins blooming in its first season. In zone 9a, plants freeze back in hard winters but resprout reliably from the root crown in spring. In zone 9b-10, it grows as a reliable semi-evergreen perennial shrub blooming nearly year-round. Prune by one-third after each bloom flush to maintain compact form and stimulate reblooming. Roots readily from stem cuttings. An excellent hummingbird plant.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Mexican Honeysuckle blooms most prolifically in full sun but performs well in partial shade in zone 9 and actually benefits from afternoon shade during the peak of summer heat. Justicia spicigera is a large, soft-stemmed, fast-growing shrub from Mexico and Central America in the Acanthaceae family, closely related to the more commonly grown Jacobinia (Justicia carnea) and Shrimp Plant (Justicia brandegeana). The species' common name 'Mexican Honeysuckle' refers not to any botanical relationship with true Lonicera honeysuckles but to the nectar-rich tubular flowers, which are intensely attractive to hummingbirds and butterflies — the long, tubular, orange to orange-red flowers emerging from softly fuzzy, velvety calyxes are among the most reliable hummingbird attractants in the zone 9 garden. The flowers are produced in terminal spikes and bloom over an exceptionally long season, essentially year-round in zone 9b-10 with brief pauses between flush cycles. The large, soft, gray-green, velvety leaves give the plant an attractive textural quality even out of bloom. In Mexican folk medicine, Justicia spicigera leaves (Mohintli) have traditional uses as a blue dye plant and herbal remedy. Among the most underused but most hummingbird-effective shrubs for Gulf Coast partial-shade garden beds.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Justicia spicigera: Mexican Honeysuckle
University of Texas at Austin Native Plant Database — Justicia spicigera
LSU AgCenter — Hummingbird shrubs for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Justicia spicigera for Gulf Coast hummingbird gardens
